source: BEP Research — synthesized from observed traffic patterns at the workload types listed below. Token estimates are typical-case medians, not maxima.

workloads.name: Code completion (Cursor / Copilot style)
workloads.description: Single inline completion: editor context + open files prefix, short generation.
workloads.exampleCallout: Copilot reports ~330ms p50 latency on completions — the latency tier matters here.

workloads.name: RAG Q&A on a 50-page document
workloads.description: Retrieve 5–10 chunks, stuff into prompt, generate a structured answer with citations.
workloads.exampleCallout: Cost is dominated by the input side. Input-token pricing matters more than output-token pricing.

workloads.name: Coding agent task (Claude Code / Cursor agent)
workloads.description: Multi-turn agent reading & writing files, running tools, iterating until task complete.
workloads.exampleCallout: Long-context flagship territory. 100k input is conservative — real agent runs blow well past that.

workloads.name: Customer support agent (5-turn conversation)
workloads.description: RAG into help-center docs + user history, multi-turn back-and-forth to resolve a ticket.
workloads.exampleCallout: Cumulative tokens per ticket. Tier of model matters less than reliability + latency.

workloads.name: Voice agent — single exchange
workloads.description: Streaming low-latency back-and-forth. Time-to-first-token matters as much as $/M.
workloads.exampleCallout: Fast-tier variants (gpt-oss-120b-fast, Kimi-K2.5-fast etc.) are priced for this workload.
